data.title: It's Not 1999: The AI 'Bubble' Has Lots Of Room To Run
data.description:
Thesis Summary

For the last year, people have been screaming about an AI bubble.

Yes, AI equities have rallied hard, and expectations around AI may have exceeded reality.

But that doesn’t mean we are in a bubble, and even if we are, we could still be very far from popping it.

Looking at actual comparisons of the most recent tech bubble, 1999, and today shows that from a fundamental perspective, there’s still a lot more air to be blown into this bubble.

This Is Not 1999

While everyone wants to compare this AI rally to the 1999 tech bubble, there are just too many differences, even today.

Cash Flow vs Capex

A lot has been said about the divergence between the expansion in Capex and the reduction Free cash flow.

While this is true, and it’s something that could be problematic for select companies, the overall tech market is very far from the levels seen in the 90s.

The ratio of Capex-FCF is below 40%, and this is mainly due to the very strong cash flows from the big players.

As we can see, the operating cash flow of the big tech companies still far exceeds Capital Expenditures.

High-Yield Debt Growth

But James, haven’t companies been issuing bonds to pay for this new AI infrastructure rollout? Indeed, they have, and these include megacaps like Meta (META) and Alphabet (GOOGL).

But again, a simple look at 1999 comparables shows that we are likely very far from any danger zone.

The chart above shows high-yield and investment-grade debt growth during 1996-2001 and between 2022 and 2025.

And as we can see, debt use exploded ahead of the market top in 2000. Very different story from today, when debt has grown 9% in 2025.

Are we accelerating? Clearly, yes, but this doesn’t look like a problem

AI productivity

Lastly, though this is harder to quantify, and we have to be skeptical of any projections made today, the pace of AI adoption and productivity growth seems to be outperforming that of the internet.

For starters, from a fundamental perspective, I see AI as a much more natural evolution from today’s technological level.

The internet revolution was jarring and met with significant resistance. But we have lived in a tech-first world for two decades now, and AI is a natural extension that is being embraced by individuals and companies alike.

Adoption continues to increase amongst companies, with nearly 10% of companies now saying they used AI tools in the last two weeks.

And according to Accenture, productivity gains in the US will reach 35% by 2035. We should take this with a grain of salt, but we also can’t deny there is clear evidence of increased productivity across many sectors.

The Risk Factor

The real risk factor now is not that companies go bankrupt in a 1999-style capitulation. Rather, the real risk factor is that the AI buildout slows down due to bottlenecks and supply constraints.

Companies are trading based on ambitious plans that these data centers will be built out, and if this timeline is pushed back, valuations will reflect this.

I’m talking about limitations in commodities, for example, or in certain components, like memory, which has recently been in the news.

A slowdown of the infrastructure rollout could temper enthusiasm and valuations, but if anything, it could also serve to dampen animal spirits and lead to a more balanced AI rollout.

How To Actually Profit

Now, here’s the key issue for investors.

In 2024, the market was chasing AI infrastructure names as everyone rushed to build out data centers. But competition has intensified, and valuations are now high.

Ultimately, I see companies building out data centers as comparable to those laying down fiber optic wire.

They are putting down key infrastructure that will facilitate the next technological revolution, but that doesn’t mean they will be the primary beneficiaries.

Those who rose to fame during the dot-com and its aftermath were those who could successfully build applications on top of the internet layer.

Similarly, the AI winners will be those leveraging the technology, not laying out the infrastructure.

In some cases, they may be one and the same: Google and Meta.

In other cases, it could be pioneers in the sector, like Palantir

And probably, the largest AI-enabled companies of the next decade are working out of a garage right now.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, I don’t think there is clear evidence of an AI bubble.

However, certain AI-related sectors may have run too far and will underperform in 2026.

But AI is still where the real opportunities for high growth and returns are.

data.title: S&P 500: Why 2026 Is A Bad Year To Do 60-40
data.description:
How 60-40 Worked In The Past

As all major equity indices (such as the DJI, SP500, NASDAQ, and RTY) started 2026 at near record prices, I have been asked by several friends about the 60-40 portfolio (i.e., a portfolio consisting of 60 percent equities and 40 percent fixed income). I can totally see the reasons behind their interest at this juncture. First, equity valuation is quite elevated, which makes bonds relatively cheap given their current yield (and I will elaborate more in the third section on this point). Second, the 60-40 model has historically served as the gold standard for balanced investing and worked beautifully. It is easier for investors to find comfort in a time-proven model.

Against this backdrop, the goals of this analysis are threefold. First, I want to explain why the 60-40 fixed allocation model has worked well in the past. Second, I will identify the top reasons to cause poor results in 2026 from this model given the current equity and bond market conditions. And finally, I will propose a few alternative ideas besides fixed templated like 60-40, as a diagnosis without a treatment plan does not help too much.

The primary reasons for 60-40’s success are twofold in my view. First, the negative correlation between the two asset classes allows investors to exploit the volatilities in them, allowing – forcing is a better way in my view – them to buy low and sell high on both assets. I certainly expected this factor to keep working in 2026 and beyond, and one of my alternative ideas (dynamic allocation) precisely seeks to exploit this factor. Although the second factor, which is the key factor in the past 3~4 decades behind 60-40’s success in my view, is the secular decline of treasury rates. As you can see from the following chart, the overall rate environment began its falling trendline in the early 1980s, when rates peaked at ~16% and steadily declined to be below 1% during the epic easing amid the COVID pandemic. A 15% yield decline roughly translates to 150% for 10-year bonds, and more on bonds with longer duration in terms of price gain. The total return would be even better when the coupons are included (and even better when reinvested). This is a key reason for this strategy’s impressive annualized return (often in the double digits) in the past.

60-40 Likely To Work Poorly In 2026

But facts have changed since then, and so should our investing strategies. The 60-40 portfolio has started to lose its magic in recent years. As an example, the chart below provides some statistics during two time frames to illustrate the significant shift in its risk and return profile over the years. From 2011 to 2021, the strategy delivered a decent annual return of around 9% per annum (again mostly due to the decline of bond rates) and relatively low volatility of less than 8%. However, in the post-2022 regime, the strategy delivered a return of less than 6% per annum while suffering far higher volatilities (almost 12%).

I see large odds for the poor performance since 2022 to continue into 2026 for several reasons. The top one is the real interest rates. Given the persistent inflation (which I expect to last longer), the current treasury rates are near the lowest levels since the 1970s. To wit, as shown in the next chart, the real interest rates hovered around a historical average rate of 3.70% most of the time during the past 4~5 decades. The reading dropped sharply since around 2020 to the current level of -1.26%, not only far below the long-term average but also near the most negative levels since the 1960s (the historical low observed during this period is -1.28% in the mid-1970s, as seen). Having substantial exposure to fixed-income instruments with a negative real yield is simply a losing strategy.

Second, unlike in the 1970s or 1980s, the current level of treasury rates has far less room to drop (even if they do drop). As shown earlier, 10-year treasury rates currently hover around 4.2%. If they drop to 1% again like amid the pandemic – which happened once only in recorded history to my knowledge, the drop would roughly cause a 32% price gain. Next, I will explain why I see similar return potential from equity but with far better odds in the next few years.

Alternative Ideas

Before moving further and offering my alternative ideas, let me emphasize that I am not trying to argue that the current equity market is cheap. What I am trying to argue is that bonds are not cheap either; thus, a fixed exposure as large as 40% is a poor strategy. To illustrate these points, the chart below shows the S&P 500’s excess P/E yield relative to 10-year treasury rates. As of this writing, the SP500 is priced at a P/E ratio of around 31.4x, translating into an earnings yield of 3.18%. With 10-year treasury rates around 4.2%, the spread between the S&P 500’s earnings yield and the 10-year treasury rates is about -1%. As shown in the chart below, this is considerably thinner than the long-term average of around 0.74% (shown by the dashed red line), indicating expensive equity valuation.

However, I expect the SP500’s earnings to grow at robust rates, and thus the FWD earning yield is much better. For instance, based on consensus EPS estimates, the FWD P/E ratio for the SP500 is currently about 22.4x. Compared with the current P/E of 31.4x, this implies an earnings growth rate of almost 40%. Such a growth rate could certainly be too aggressive. But to echo a point, I’ve made earlier, I see far larger odds of equity earnings growth and price again than bond price appreciation. The growth expectation could also thicken the excess yield materially. Assuming a 22.4x FWD P/E ratio and a slightly lower 10-year rate of 4% in 2026, SP500’s excess yield could widen to about 0.46%, as shown in the chart above, quite close to the historical average.

With this, let me offer a few alternative investment ideas for 2026, as illustrated and also implemented in our own holdings, as shown in the chart. First, we suggest far lower bond exposure than 40% (our current bond exposure is around 24.2%, as seen). Second, we suggest (and have been making the same suggestion over the past few years) investors consider an allocation toward precious metals. As seen, our current target allocation toward gold and silver is 7%. As detailed in our recent articles dedicated to gold and silver, I keep seeing a favorable return/risk ratio here despite their strong price rallies in 2025.

Third, we suggest investors consider a dynamic allocation model rather than a fixed template like the 60-40. Fixed allocation templates in general make little sense to me, especially during critical market junctures, which often are junctures that define long-term investment returns. The market conditions during the epic easing following the breakout of the COVID pandemic serve as a most recent example (but not the only one, though). At that time, as aforementioned, 10-year treasury rates dropped below 1%, and treasury rates with shorter terms dropped to almost zero. If you follow the 60-40 or any other fixed allocation template, you will still be holding a good amount of bonds. While under those conditions, holding treasury bonds only brings return-free risks to your portfolio instead of risk-free returns.

For us, the valuation dynamics between bonds and the SP500 shown in the chart above have served as a simple yet effective model over the years. To wit, the next two charts below show our aggressive growth portfolio’s performance since we started posting it as part of our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement) in 2022 and also the longer-term performance. In the third chart, our portfolio is code-named Leveraged All-Weather Portfolio ('AWP') because A) its core holdings resemble those of Ray Dalio’s 4-asset model, and B) it includes a leveraged twist (with TQQQ).

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

As a final idea under the current market conditions and an ideal to also implement in our own portfolio, investors could further split the equity market into different segments and dynamically allocate exposures among these segments. This idea is implemented in our portfolio via tactical holdings, where we look for more valuation-oriented holdings to counterbalance the growth-dominated core holdings. These days, overall market funds (VOO, SPY, and our VTI holding) are all dominated by growth-oriented tech firms.

Before closing, I feel a warning about TQQQ is in order. The past performance of our portfolio has been driven partially by the inclusion of TQQQ in our portfolio (even though we don’t currently have any exposure to it). We adjust our TQQQ exposure based on the valuation risk in QQQ and also the VIX index, as detailed in an earlier article dedicated to TQQQ. Readers need to be aware of the structural risks TQQQ entails. Its fee is relatively high compared to large index funds. It does not pay dividends either, even though its underlying assets (say, QQQ) do. It suffers a volatility decay, which means if QQQ keeps fluctuating sideways, TQQQ would suffer a loss. As such, we allocate our TQQQ exposure dynamically and only hold it tactically.

To close, the goal of this article is to caution investors against the use of the 60-40 template under current market conditions. We feel the cliché that “past performance does not guarantee future returns” is very timely for the 60-40 template with record low real rates and limited price appreciation upside for treasury bonds. It is true that equities are expensive, but they are not as expensive as they appear on the surface once we factor in their growth and their excess return to bond yields (especially real bond yields). As such, instead of 60-40, we invited investors to consider some alternative ideas such as dynamic allocation, alternative assets such as precious metals, and the use of tactical holdings to supplement their SP500 exposure.

data.title: HA Sustainable Infrastructure: Capacity Needs Outweigh Policy Headwinds
data.description:
Introduction

HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital, Inc. (HASI) has been on a decent run this past year, and I don’t think this is going to change in 2026 or even 2027. I admit the power-hungry U.S. is going to continue to provide the need for capacity, and no policies are going to completely offset the growth in electrification needs of tomorrow. HASI is a Buy.

Briefly on Performance

Q3 ’25 quarter for the company was a record-setting one, with GAAP EPS coming in at $0.61 vs $-0.17 last year, and adjusted EPS up 54% to $0.80. In terms of portfolio and investments, the company closed around $650m of investments in Q3, and $1.5B YTD, which yield over 10.5%. This helped the company’s adjusted recurring net investment income grow at around 42% y/y to $105m, and total revenue to $103m. Managed assets grew 15% to $15B, while reiterating guidance for adjusted EPS CAGR of 8%-10% through 2027, with 10% projected for 2025.

The company’s pipeline remains robust at over $6B, even if we take into account the recent $1.2B structured equity investment in the 2.6GW SunZia utility-scale renewable project via a joint venture with KKR (KKR).

So, we can see the company has had a great momentum in the last while, driven by the ever-increasing demand for energy across the U.S., helping the company maintain its robust pipeline, increase managed assets by mid-teens % growth, and increase its portfolio yield by 50bps to 8.6%. The company is on pace to exceed $3B in investments for the rest of the year. The recent SunZia deal will net the company around $600m in positive balance sheet impact, which will help it maintain its above-average ROE, which has been growing at a steady rate.

I was wrong - President Trump cannot do anything to stop the momentum

In my previous article on the company, I argued that Trump’s “drill, baby, drill” rhetoric was going to negatively impact the company’s performance. It seems that I did not take into account how power-hungry, and I mean that in a literal and figurative sense, the U.S. is going to be going forward. There’s only so much drilling you can do before turning to renewables to make up for the lack of energy creation. The U.S. desperately needs substantial new energy capacity to meet the surging demand, which is forecasted to hit record highs in ’25 and ’26. Electricity consumption in the U.S. is set to rise to more than 4,260B kilowatt-hours in 2026, up almost 4%. The increase is driven by the usual suspects of the last couple of years, which are data centers and AI workloads, and sprinkled on top are some of the manufacturing reshoring, as well as overall electrification efforts.

Sure, Trump may not like renewables and would prefer to boost fossil fuels, but like it or not, the only way to meet this demand for energy is to collectively work with all types of energy, renewable and otherwise, if he wants the U.S. to maintain a leadership position in the next technological revolution, which is AI.

The newest headwind for the company is the One Big Beautiful Bill, which moved forward with phasing out many IRA-era clean energy tax credits and tightening the rules, as I warned in my first article. However, due to the clean energy buildout, HASI’s growth remained intact because a lot of the policy headwinds were offset due to the structural U.S. power demand.

The management has been downplaying the impact of clean energy tax credit phaseouts and said that these will take several years to really impact anyone, which gives the company ample time to keep expanding its reach across different assets in the future, which will help offset, and potentially override, the policy risks. This can be seen from the company’s very positive projections for the next two years at least. I am leaning towards this outcome as well. These changes will make the company’s growth somewhat worse, but overall, given how well diversified the company is right now, and how it is looking to continue to expand its investment in other asset classes, the impact will be minimal.

The most promising “Next Frontier”- for a few years

As I mentioned already, HASI is very well diversified across many different asset classes, which limits concentration risks. Now, we can see that there are many more asset classes that the company is looking to dip its toes in, and one of them, I think, will be the winner in the short-to-medium term that will help the company maintain momentum and increase operational performance further, and that is Data Centers.

It’s interesting how the company is framing data centers as an asset class on its own. That is mostly due to how much scale and innovation potential the company sees in data centers. The potential remains massive, and still increasing, as we saw from all the hyperscalers continuing their generational AI race, with billions more to be spent on top of the already inflated AI capex spend for 2025. It’s honestly ridiculous what is going on, but who am I to question where to make money? The money is in data centers now, so HASI will have a few good years on the back of data centers, and by then, there will be some other next innovation.

The next frontier represents around 9% of the total pipeline, but what this is missing is the fact that grid-connected, which is 29% as of Q3 ’25, already directly and indirectly fuels hyperscalers through large-scale, contracted renewable energy projects that supply power to data centers. For example, the recent $1.2B SunZia transaction delivers renewables to high-demand regions like California, New Mexico, and Texas, which are the regions where hyperscalers cluster. And if the future energy needs are for residential or some other needs besides AI and data centers, it is still going to be because of data centers hogging all the energy output. It’s all connected. The recent joint venture between HASI and Sunrun (RUN) to accelerate the buildout of residential solar and battery systems, I think, only proves that it is very much necessary to accelerate capacity buildouts because otherwise, data centers will take all the capacity from everywhere else.

Risks

The company has a decent amount of debt on its books that is floating-rate debt. The longer the interest rates remain high, the more it has to pay out in interest expense. I don’t think we will see a hike in interest rates anytime soon, but I also don’t think we will ever see the lows in interest rates as we saw pre-COVID, which were ultra-low. A new norm of 3% or so is going to prevail, in my opinion, unless something tragic happens to the U.S. economy.

The big news of the weekend was the news that federal prosecutors opened a criminal investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell regarding the central bank’s renovation project, saying that Powell lied about the project’s scope. Powell responded immediately and said that this attack has nothing to do with the project and more with Trump’s dislike of Powell, and that he is taking too long to lower rates. Which is not a surprise at all. So, is there a chance we will see lower rates quicker? I don’t think so. Powell remains independent and will not be phased by this investigation. But in the off chance that does change things, lower interest rates are positive for HASI. What happens to the economy after that is unknown, but my guess is that inflation will return once again, repeating the cycle again.

Further tariff talks could impact the supply chain of the pipeline, especially for Chinese solar panels and Mexico/Canada components, which could inflate costs, but if the pipeline sources materials from the U.S., then it will have a limited impact overall.

Data center growth will eventually begin to subside. There’s only so much money the hyperscaler can shovel into them before seeing lower returns than expected. Then, I expect money to dry out, and valuations fall.

Closing Comments

My view of the company has changed quite a bit. I think this run still has at least a couple of more years of gains before we see a slowdown and correction. Once the data center juice runs out, there will no doubt be another promising venture. Something new, completely, or just the much bigger push towards electrification of the globe under a new administration after Trump. Unless Trump was serious about trying for a third term. I jest of course, but you never know what happens in three years.

So, if there is still money and growth to be had for the next couple of years, I am upgrading the company to a Buy.

data.title: EPAM Systems: Bullish About Financial Improvement Potential
data.description:
I've stayed positive on EPAM Systems, Inc. (EPAM).

A more rapid pace of growth and higher margins are what I'm predicting for EPAM this year. Its shares are attractively priced based on peer and historical comparisons.

The Oct. 28, 2025, initiation piece highlighted EPAM's distribution of surplus capital to stockholders and my favorable expectations of its 3Q25 performance.

Collaboration With Tech Leaders Is An Edge

EPAM's Jan. 8, 2026, media release revealed a "strategic partnership with Cursor, a leading AI-powered development platform, to accelerate enterprise adoption of generative AI engineering for clients."

The EPAM-Cursor Deal's Benefits

The firm boasts an alliance of over 150 members. Apart from the recently added Cursor, the list includes Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL).

At its 3Q25 briefing on Nov. 6, 2025, EPAM noted that it rolled out "AI voice agents that handle over 100,000 calls weekly" by utilizing MSFT's Azure. The MSFT-Azure co-operation also led to the creation of digital co-pilots for a customer, as mentioned below.

Dutch Retailer's New AI-Related Initiative

Last month, the firm disclosed "the availability of several new, high-impact AI agents on Google Cloud Marketplace."

The GOOG-EPAM Ecosystem's Latest Solutions

Separately, it has a recent offering on the "Amazon Web Services/AWS" Marketplace called "EPAM AI/Run for AWS Migration and Modernization."

EPAM's New AWS-Related Product

A Dec. 2, 2025, announcement indicated that EPAM "co-built several AI-enabled solutions" with AMZN, which includes the above-mentioned.

In my opinion, these relationships are a win-win situation.

EPAM's value proposition for partners lies with its role as "client zero for AI adoption" as per the 3Q2025 call commentary. The group emphasized at the results meeting that it's "actually trying it out and experimenting on ourselves" before pushing the AI-focused products into the market.

On the other hand, it gets access to the internet giants' most advanced models without being beholden to any single platform.

The financial numbers suggest that EPAM's ecosystem-led strategy is working well. According to its early-November earnings briefing, 3Q25 marked the "third consecutive quarter of double-digit sequential growth" for the company's "pure AI-native revenues."

Grabbing A Bigger Slice Of The Pie

EPAM met with Wells Fargo's (WFC) analysts in the previous month. Details of the conversation were outlined in a Dec. 19, 2025, WFC report (not publicly available) titled "Takeaways From IR Catch-ups." What caught my eye was the firm's remarks on "increased wallet share among clients, resulting in incremental revenues growth."

Also, there are encouraging read-throughs from sales mix changes.

Turnover derived from non-top-20 clients came in +24% higher YoY in the 3rd quarter of FY25. That was better than the +10% growth of the top-20 during the same period. It's clear that EPAM is expanding beyond the core base.

The 3Q25 call commentary pointed to EPAM being a share-gainer due to customers "redirecting work" from rivals "who successfully sold advanced capability, but failed to deliver it."

EPAM also took part in JPMorgan's (JPM) forum late last year. JPM published a research note (inaccessible in public) with the name "Ultimate Services Investor Conference Takeaways" on Nov. 18, 2025.

At JPM's event, management stressed that its "clients that left (previously) and have returned should now stay" thanks to the group's "better execution" relative to peers. These recaptured conversions are indicative of its share gain potential.

It's no surprise that EPAM's growth prospects are promising.

The company expects its topline increase to improve from FY24's +1% to FY2025's +15%. It also thinks that the FY26 "organic growth rate will be higher than" last year (source: earnings call).

Multiple Levers To Meet Updated Margin Target

Last November, EPAM raised the mid-point of its FY25 normalized EBIT-to-sales guide from 15.00% to 15.15%. As per S&P Capital IQ, the consensus anticipates that its EBIT-to-revenue will rise further to 15.50% for the current year.

My take is that the profitability enhancement is achievable.

EPAM has been adding "organic headcount across key locations, such as India, Central Eastern Europe, and South America" based on its Q3 call disclosures. Recruiting in places with less expensive labor than the U.S. will help to shrink its costs.

Also, its workforce used to be top-heavy, focusing on senior hires. Going forward, the enterprise's plan is to attract more junior staff and train them well. That is likely to optimize EPAM's overall wage structure.

Separately, the firm talked about "being more picky and more selective on the deals" and its expectations of "price increases" for '26 at the third quarter briefing.

EPAM is prioritizing high-value work that can command good margins.

As discussed in the preceding section, it is taking business away from less competent competitors. This should give it the bargaining power to charge more for its services.

You Need To Contemplate These Risks

The pace of AI adoption might slow in the future to the detriment of EPAM. That can happen if end-users don't witness a jump in productivity after executing on AI-related projects.

EPAM could face cost-down pressures. This assumes that corporations become more budget-conscious in a tough economic environment.

The Final Word

The stock is still a "Buy"-rated name in my view.

EPAM trades at a 12 times consensus next 12 months' EV/EBIT as per S&P Capital IQ. The 10-year mean was 22x.

Its listed comparable Accenture's (ACN) forward EBIT ratio is 15x.

EPAM's EV-EBIT can possibly re-rate by at least +25% (15/12) benchmarked with its peer. I see top-line acceleration and wider margins in FY2026 as catalysts.

data.title: Here's Why IREN Is An Incredibly Speculative Gamble, Not An Investment
data.description:
As we turn into 2026, the stock market and the U.S. economy continues to be one big bet on artificial intelligence (AI). There are so many ways that investors are playing this theme at this point, it’s sometimes hard to keep track. One very popular theme, though, is the so-called “neoclouds” – AI-facilitating cloud computing service providers. In this space, IREN Limited (IREN) is a popular choice amongst the AI bulls. In this article, I’m going to argue that, contrary to what the bulls would have you believe, IREN’s future prospects over the next 5 years or so are pretty bleak. There are many flashing red lights for IREN investors that are currently clearly visible, and, as a result, investors should look elsewhere for a place to put their capital.

“Black Box” First Principles

When it comes to analyzing the future prospects of a company like IREN, based on the analysis that I’m seeing elsewhere, I think it’s super important to come back to investing first principles. A helpful way to do this is to strip out all the complexity and to get down to the essence of the thing. One can think of a business as being a black box that (hopefully) spits out cash from time to time. The more cash your black box spits out, the more valuable it is. However, if your black box doesn’t spit out cash, it has no value. It’s like a rock that just sits there and does nothing. Even worse, though, if your black box functions like a black hole and sucks up and destroys cash from the outside world, it has negative value and is the worst sort of thing from an investor’s perspective.

So, I think, of all these outcomes, it’s clear what kind of thing investors want their black boxes to be – namely, things that just gush cash. These are the types of investments to which investors should and do ascribe the most value in most cases. However, from time to time, investors get caught up in the complexities of the future prospects of a business and forget this fundamental, first-principle framework – namely, if you don’t have compelling reason to think the thing is going to be spitting out cash at an appropriate rate at sometime in the sufficiently near future, you probably shouldn’t be ascribing much value to that thing. I think this sort of conflation and confusion of relevant factors in evaluating the business is exactly what’s going on with IREN (as well as other neoclouds, which I’ve written about here and here). This article is going to be an attempt to lay bare where the elements of that confusion are.

IREN and the Neoclouds

To best understand IREN, I think it’s important to remind ourselves of the generic AI value chain:

There are two important ways this value chain could be functioning:

  1. “Healthy” – End users (i.e., consumers and enterprises) could be demonstrating adequate demand for the industry’s products. Here “demonstrating adequate demand” means demonstrating a willingness to pay for the industry’s products in such a way that the costs of the players up the value chain are covered and there’s room for those upstream players to make a profit based off the dollars flowing into the value chain from this end user demand.
  2. “Broken” – End users might not be demonstrating adequate demand (as just defined) and, instead, the upstream players survive (and in some cases thrive – e.g., Nvidia [NVDA]) based on dollars being passed around in a circle between the upstream players together with outside injections of capital to this circular party from venture capital, private debt markets, etc.

Without question, we are currently in a regime where the second scenario, the “broken” scenario best describes what is going on. This important fact is crucial to remember as we think about the future prospects of the neoclouds in general, and IREN specifically. If the current regime doesn’t transition to more of a “healthy” dynamic, and doesn’t do so in a sufficiently timely manner, the whole ecosystem could experience significant deterioration in the face of economic reality.

For those who don’t know, neoclouds are a new type of cloud computing provider that has emerged in the wake of the current AI bubble in which we find ourselves. Intuitively speaking, they can be thought of as GPU-as-a-service providers, allowing AI model makers, developers, and enterprises to rent out GPU’s for their various AI applications. As such, these guys are clearly in the “Data Center” node of the above value chain.

Now, the various neoclouds put their own idiosyncratic spins on this; some players are fully integrated, some own the GPU’s and lease the data centers in which they operate, various players have various levels of software applications they offer on top of their hardware offerings, etc. There are some analysts in this space who make big distinctions between the different neoclouds based on these nuances; however, I think this sort of analysis should largely be disregarded as it strikes me as missing the forest for the trees. To anticipate the discussion to come later, these analysts strike me as missing the key point that these neocloud players look to be operating in a structurally bad business, and, as a result, it doesn’t seem terribly relevant whether this one has this minute advantage over here or that one has that minute advantage over there. Their collective fortunes are likely to be determined by the same macro waves which will, in time, make fully evident just how bad these businesses truly are.

Nevertheless, just for the sake of a deeper understanding, if not anything else, it’s important to understand where IREN stands in this regard. IREN differentiates itself from the other neoclouds by being a fully-integrated player, meaning it owns the land, the shell, the power connection, the GPU’s, etc. Some think this model offers IREN a significant competitive advantage, an observation that allows us to segue to a discussion of the bull-case for IREN.

The Bull-Case for IREN

The bull-case for IREN has two primary components, as I see it – industry tailwinds and business-specific advantages.

The industry tailwinds component is largely something most people are aware of. In short, it’s the idea that “AI is the future,” and the only way to keep making significant advancements in AI is to follow the predictions of the so-called “scaling laws” which say that advancement in models and increasing levels of compute go hand-in-hand. Thus, McKinsey predicts we’re going to need to have $7 trillion of capex in this space by 2030, and Sam Altman thinks we’re going to end up covering the earth with data centers and eventually will be putting data centers in space.

The business-specific component of the IREN bulls' thesis seems to largely hinge on this idea that IREN is a fully integrated player owning the full-stack. The IREN bulls point to the slow-down and counter-party risk that comes along with not being fully integrated in this way – cf. CoreWeave’s (CRWV) recent hit to its stock price resulting from delays from a third-party data center developer. Additionally, the IREN bulls point to the ~3GW of secured grid-connected power in a space where access to power is revealing itself to be a very important factor:

As the above alludes to, there’s also the industry validation offered by the company’s recently won $9.7 billion deal with Microsoft. What’s more, there’s IREN’s previously honed infrastructure and operational expertise from the bitcoin mining business it’s currently in the process of pivoting away from, which, the bulls argue, should transfer to this new AI infrastructure-provisioning business. Finally, as with most of the neoclouds, the IREN bulls pound the table about the prospect for rapid revenue growth over the next several years.

While the bull case for IREN might sound somewhat compelling at a first glance, I think it pretty easily falls apart upon further scrutiny. Let’s turn there next.

The Bear Case for IREN

The first fundamental thing to underscore about IREN from a bearish perspective is just how incredibly speculative their future business prospects currently are. As such, their previous financial statements, which largely reflect their bitcoin mining activities, offer minimal insight into their future prospects as a neocloud.

As we can see here, per the latest income statement from the most recent 10-Q, Bitcoin mining revenues account for roughly 97% of total sales. Even still, the Bitcoin mining business itself is not profitable from an operational income perspective, with -31.8% operating margins in the most recent quarter. Furthermore, the company had negative free cash flows:

And this general story of negative operating margins and free cash flows has been true of the company's operations for years now. So, in a certain sense, perhaps shareholders should rejoice at the prospect of Bitcoin mining being less and less of the focus for the company going forward.

When we turn to the balance sheet, while the debt position is currently comfortable with a debt/equity ratio of ~0.33, it is important to note that the company has had a history of aggressively issuing shares:

This is an important point from the perspective of what to expect for shareholders going forward. In my opinion, the company is going to need significant access to capital markets in the coming years to fund its rapid scaling efforts. This likely translates to significant further dilution of current shareholders or the conversion of a debt position that currently looks tenable to one that might turn out not to be so, depending on how things play out. In either case, this is not a future I'd be looking forward to as a shareholder.

So, with this by way of backdrop, to get a sense for the company's prospects going forward, especially given its high-stakes transition from Bitcoin miner to neocloud, I think we need a new frame of reference for what to expect beyond looking at IREN's past financial statements.

Other neoclouds offer insight in this regard. One thing I was struck by in compiling my recent write-ups on Nebius and Coreweave was just how bad of a light the current financial statements were casting these companies’ operations in. The TLDR here was money losing businesses that incinerate cash, are highly capital-intensive, and which are highly reliant on capital markets (with the attendant risks that come along with this of significant shareholder dilution or the taking on of potentially crippling levels of debt) to fuel their growth.

“Well,” you say, “that’s just now. In a few years down the road …” … And that’s precisely the point. The bullish argument is always “in a few years down the road, everything’s going to be sunshine and rainbows.” Meanwhile the current numbers suggest these businesses incinerate cash and they don’t have operational leverage as they scale (detailed extensively in my articles on CRWV and NBIS). But operational leverage is precisely the thing they need as they grow, otherwise there’s no basis for hope with these businesses. As I underscore in the Nebius and CoreWeave write-ups, if they don’t produce operational leverage as they scale, it doesn’t matter how much they scale revenue, they still produce no value for shareholders. In my CoreWeave article, I use an analogy of a pizza shop that scales its operations by selling pizzas well below cost. The point was that, even though there was a lot of demand (selling things well below their cost of production can have this effect), there was no value created (for the owner; a lot of value was created for the customers) in that business. And this gets back to the “black box” metaphor from the beginning of this article. If there is no plausible prospective path towards profitability, it doesn’t matter how much or how quickly IREN (or any of the other neoclouds) scale their operations, the business operations themselves (as opposed to the infrastructure, land, etc., which could be repurposed or resold in a fire sale or the like) have no inherent value. Thus, when people pound the table about P/S figures two or three years out, they are just missing the point, as rapidly scaling sales says nothing about rapidly scaling profitability or even the prospect of profitability at all.

And it makes sense when one examines the structural headwinds that these neoclouds face.

  • First, again, they are incredibly capital-intensive. Highly capital-intensive businesses have, statistically speaking, proven themselves throughout history to be much more likely to produce comparatively little in the way of shareholder value (especially when compared to capital-light businesses). Given the massively speculative transition in operations IREN is currently undergoing, it doesn’t help at all that the unproven business to which they’re transitioning has this significant strike against it.
  • Their operating costs are also very high when you factor in everything involved – power costs, cooling costs, depreciation costs, etc. Depreciation costs, in particular, represent a huge wild card, given the ongoing debate about the useful lives of GPU’s operating continuously near peak capacity in these data centers. Even in the case that the GPU’s have useful lives of six years, it’s not obvious that the economics can work here, and, if their useful lives are something like 2-3 years, as many have compellingly argued, the whole prospect of making the economics work really just falls flat on its face.
  • They’re getting squeezed on all sides. Their suppliers (e.g., Nvidia) have huge pricing power. Their customers (i.e., the hyperscalers) have massive leverage over them when it comes to contract negotiations, given the customer concentration component. And, they face intense competition with each other.

All-in-all, this looks like an extremely unattractive area in which to operate. As such, it doesn’t surprise me that the hyperscalers are looking to offload these services to someone else rather than standing these businesses up themselves. The bulls in this space argue that the hyperscalers are compute-constrained and, thus, they are forced to offload their compute requirements to other players like the neoclouds, and that this is a positive sign for the neoclouds’ business. They never seem to consider the opposite state of affairs – namely, maybe the hyperscalers just don’t want these operations on their books, so they’re looking to offload the operations to third parties as much as possible, thus making space in the competitive landscape for the neoclouds. If these were truly amazing businesses, the hyperscalers have all the resources they would need to big-foot out these smaller players and take all the riches for themselves. My guess is that the hyperscalers see that there’s not much value in this space, so better to offload it to third parties as much as possible. This way, the hyperscalers get all the benefits of the latest compute capacity (both now, next year, the following year, etc.), but don’t have to undergo all the capex intensity involved, notably foregoing the need to pay for the actual constant refreshes of expensive Nvidia chips.

In short, based on all the evidence we have so far, provisioning AI-facilitating compute has no compelling evidence of being an economically attractive area within which to operate. Just because “neocloud” and traditional “cloud computing” (where, for many people, the truly stunning shot-in-the-arm AWS gave to Amazon in its ascendance comes to mind) have the word “cloud” in them, it doesn’t follow that the economics of a legacy cloud computing business like AWS translates over to these neoclouds.

Putting It All Together

I don’t think it’s obvious at all that the current AI data center buildout continues over the next 5-10 years in line with consensus expectations (see section “Scenario 1: AI Buildout Fails to Continue at the Expected ‘Fever-Pitch’ Rate” of my recent article on AMD for a more detailed discussion of this). If this is true, the massive tailwinds expected from it for IREN and the other neocloud players will not materialize.

Further, I think the evidence we currently have now of how bad of a business area provisioning AI-facilitating compute capacity is will prove out over time, overturning the “sunshine and rainbows” optimism of the bulls in this space. What’s more, I think the supposed competitive differentiation between players in this space – e.g., whether you’re fully integrated like IREN, or whether you just own the chips and lease out the data centers from third parties like CoreWeave – will mean comparatively little when weighed against the bad structural business economics characteristic of this space.

As a result, I think IREN bulls, in a highly speculative way, are betting on a company embarking upon a very risky transition into a business area with minimal prospect for riches (profits, not simply scaling of revenues) that promises to be highly capital-intensive, and, thus, potentially highly dilutive (in the case of requiring significant further share sales) or bringing on of material risk in the form of significant leverage (in the case of having to take on significant levels of debt). This does not at all strike me as a smart bet to make, and is, in fact, in my estimation, precisely the sort of bet investors ought to avoid.

Risks to Thesis

If consensus is right about the current trajectory of the AI infrastructure buildout over the next 5-10 years, that would make my thesis harder, as the tailwinds provided from that buildout would be there in the way the neocloud bulls are betting on. However, in such a world, there’s still the issue that this would only provide a basis to expect rapid scaling of the revenues of these businesses, not rapid scaling of profits, and, thus, shareholder value. To prove the rapid scaling of profits, the economics of these businesses would have to change dramatically from where they currently stand. While a risk to my thesis, it’s one I’m willing to bet has a low probability of coming to pass, given the current balance of the evidence. And even if on the off-chance the profits materialize for this industry, it’s not clear the extent to which IREN will participate in them, as it’s not clear the degree to which their competitive differentiation matters in such a world. While my expectation is that these neoclouds will all either largely succeed or fail together, riding the same macro waves, it could even turn out that, in a world of significant profitability, being fully integrated might even turn out to be a net-negative. I’m not saying that I think this is likely to be the case, only that it’s possible. This simply further underscores the point of the difficulty in betting on such highly speculative ventures like this one.

Investor Takeaway

There are simply too many compelling ways to imagine things not going right for IREN investors here. Going back to the discussion from the beginning, there is no compelling evidence at all that your “black box” here will spit out any meaningful amounts of cash in the future, much less that it will do so in a way that makes the current price of the stock look attractive. IREN is simply far too risky and speculative, and, thus, should be avoided. Rating: ‘strong sell’.

data.title: Tyson Foods: Protein Powerhouse Set To Benefit From The New Food‑Pyramid Shift
data.description:
Intro

If an investor is eyeing the food sector and the recent shift in consumption habits toward healthier, protein‑rich foods—further fueled just last week by the U.S. government’s updated nutrition guide—it’s hard to overlook Tyson Foods, Inc. (TSN). The company is the largest meat processor in the U.S. with about one-third of its production focused on chicken, it could be seen as the biggest winner in the protein-focused consumption trend. Combining all segments—chicken, beef, and pork—Tyson Foods stands as the largest protein producer in the country.

From USDA's latest announcement:

Prioritizing Protein: While previous Dietary Guidelines have demonized protein in favor of carbohydrates, these guidelines reflect gold standard science by prioritizing high quality, nutrient-dense protein foods in every meal. This includes a variety of animal sources, including eggs, poultry, seafood, and red meat, in addition to plant-sourced protein foods such as beans, peas, lentils, legumes, nuts, seeds, and soy.

While the company went through a challenging period a couple of years ago — including losses in fiscal 2023, scandals involving its former CFO, and the negative publicity that followed — investor sentiment understandably turned cautious. Since then, however, Tyson’s operations have found firmer footing, and its financial performance has been slowly but steadily improving. Still, the stock has recovered only modestly from its October 2023 lows.

In my view, the market has reasonably well priced in the current situation: the chicken segment is operating at full-strength and driving profitability, while challenges in the beef segment are significantly offsetting those gains. That said, I believe the market will eventually reassess once the beef segment stabilizes and moves closer to breakeven, which could materially improve the company’s overall operating profitability. And although it is widely recognized that a return to meaningful beef profitability is still years away, waiting until that point could prove too late for investors who want exposure to a clear frontrunner and leader in the ongoing shift toward protein‑focused consumption.

I take a closer look at Tyson’s prospects to assess whether now is an appropriate time to start building a position, or whether it would still be wiser to wait for a more attractive entry point.

Tyson In Brief

As the name suggests, Tyson Foods has deep roots in the Tyson family. The story began in 1931, when John W. Tyson moved to Springdale, Arkansas, in search of new opportunities and started hauling chickens to larger markets in the Midwest. That small chicken‑hauling operation eventually grew into the major meat‑processing company we know today as Tyson Foods.

These days, Tyson Foods stands out as a major player in the U.S. food industry. Headquartered in Springdale, Arkansas, the company employs roughly 133,000 people and is the largest meat producer in America. Tyson processes about one‑fifth of all the beef, chicken, and pork sold in the U.S., and it is also the country’s top beef exporter. In addition, the company owns a portfolio of well‑known brands, including Jimmy Dean, Hillshire Farm, Ball Park, Wright, Aidells, and State Fair. In short, Tyson is one of the key companies shaping the U.S. protein market.

Recent Dynamics

With chicken—its strongest category—now showing expanding profitability after several challenging, loss‑making years before finally returning to profit in 2024, as mentioned earlier, the company’s latest challenge has emerged in the beef segment, where losses have been mounting and now exceed those seen in the chicken division at its lowest point in 2023.

The company’s other major segments—Prepared Foods and Pork—have delivered relatively stable results. The Prepared Foods segment, which accounts for 18% of total sales, has been a consistently positive contributor to the bottom line, while the Pork segment, representing 11% of sales, has posted slight operating losses in recent years. The smallest of the company's segments, International, has been swinging between losses and profits, but reported a positive operating result in fiscal 2025.

More Details On The Latest Q4/FY25 Earnings

Despite the beef headwinds, Tyson’s latest fiscal year, which ended in September (the company operates on a September‑to‑September fiscal calendar), delivered solid results. Adjusted EPS of $4.12 rose 33% from the prior year, as strong performance in the chicken and international segments more than offset the pressures in beef and the small loss in pork. Total sales of $54.4 billion were up 2.1% from the prior year on a GAAP basis, and up 3.3% when excluding the impact of a $653 million increase in legal contingency accruals, which was recorded as a reduction to sales.

While Q4 revenue $13.86 billion missed estimates by $210 million, EPS of $2.13 beat the Street’s expectations by $0.32.

The company proudly highlighted its growth figures, which—though modest by general standards—look reasonably good compared to Nielsen’s reported 1.5% drop in overall U.S. food and beverage consumption.

Top performers in Tyson’s categories:

Chicken finished the year strong, having grown for four consecutive quarters YoY:

Beef, the weakest performer, continues to struggle just as it has in recent times:

While free cash flow fell short compared to last year’s performance, other financial metrics have shown improvement:

Outlook

The company doesn’t share quarterly projections, but it did provide FY26 guidance. The upper end of the revenue growth range at 2%-4% topped the analyst expectation of 2%-3%, and capital expenditures came in below the Street's estimated $1.2 billion.

Valuation

Looking at Tyson’s valuation metrics, in my view, the stock appears slightly undervalued. Its forward EV/EBITDA of 8.33 is modest in absolute terms and reflects a conservative—yet still reasonable—valuation given the uncertainties in the beef segment and the company’s modest growth outlook. Still, a relative comparison suggests a degree of undervaluation when measured against both the industry’s forward-looking average and Tyson’s own historical multiples.

Risks

One major risk, as with any food processor, is the possibility of an animal‑disease outbreak. That said, the largest producers often manage to maintain—or even improve—profitability during such periods, as they are typically better prepared for such disruptions and more capable of capitalizing on the stronger pricing environment that tends to emerge.

The second key source of risk, in my view, is the possibility that the current challenges in the beef segment persist longer than expected, which would keep TSN’s profitability under pressure for an extended period.

For food producers, commodity‑price fluctuations—especially those related to animal feed—are always a key source of risk. The current challenges in the beef segment, for example, are widely discussed as being linked to drought conditions in many regions in recent years.

Takeaway

Boosted by the U.S. government’s updated food pyramid guidelines and a broader trend toward higher protein consumption, Tyson, the nation’s largest protein processor, is positioned right at the heart of these changes. That said, the challenges in company's beef segment are not expected to ease in the near term and could take years to normalize, given the long‑cycle nature of cattle production. As a result, this segment is likely to continue weighing on the company’s bottom line.

While the food sector as a whole is facing a challenging period due to declining overall consumption—and with sugar‑heavy and more processed categories viewed unfavorably as the main losers in this shift—Tyson’s predominantly protein‑focused portfolio could be seen as a winner in the current environment.

To sum up, while I don’t consider Tyson significantly undervalued, I see it as deserving a moderate Buy rating. Explosive capital gains seem unlikely, but the company’s solid financial profile and shareholder‑friendly capital allocation—through dividends and share buybacks—could support at least market‑level returns over the longer term.

data.title: Fed Chair Powell Is Under Attack - Silver And Gold At Fresh All-Time Highs (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Donald Trump is a stubborn President, to say the least. Just when markets were enjoying a few relaxing months of silence regarding Fed independence, the Administration has struck again.

Over the weekend, the Department of Justice opened a formal investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell regarding statements made during his recent Senate testimony.

It is painfully easy to read between the lines here. This appears to be a political masquerade - a manufactured pretext to fire Powell before his term officially concludes.

The impatience seems unjustified, given that his tenure ends in May 2026 and markets are already expecting an announcement on his successor by the end of this month.

In a special address delivered yesterday evening, Chair Powell fired back, significantly upping his tone regarding the Federal Reserve's stance against politicized interference.

The verdict: market chaos.

The US dollar is plummeting to start the week, and stocks are in the red. On the other hand, keeping with the dominant trend of the past year, metals are shining bright.

Acting as the preferred safe haven - displacing even the yen and the now-compromised US Treasuries - precious metals have shot through the roof in early trading.

This political theater highlights a structural shift: Central Banks and investors are aggressively diversifying away from an overdependence on US assets as their traditionally stable reputation erodes.

Gold has now solidified its place as the largest reserve asset for central banks, with China leading a global diversification charge.

With silver breaking $85 and gold comfortably trading above its previous peak ($4,600+), the message is clear: Metals are not looking to ease their rally anytime soon.

The buying frenzy is broad-based, with platinum, palladium, copper, and aluminum all chasing new monthly or yearly highs as we speak.

Let's dive right into a two-time frame intraday analysis for gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and silver (XAGUSD:CUR) to see where these flows could be heading.

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) Daily and 2H Charts

Daily Chart

After the past week closed above $4,500, technicals went right ahead to magnify the return of even more bullish fundamentals for the yellow metal.

Since August, the rally hadn't seen many retracements, but after the late-December correction, a strong upward channel is forming, with the latest bounce being used by bulls to reach the current all-time highs.

Easily blasting beyond the weekly divergence noted last week.

Currently at $4,630 and running, gold will face a small technical resistance at $4,666, from a key Fibonacci projection (1.618 from 2023 lows to mid-2025 levels).

If bulls breach the level, there won't be much until $5,000, which coincides with the top of the channel.

Let's take a closer look.

2H Chart and Technical Levels

Forming a tight bull channel on the intraday time frame, nothing but some overbought conditions seem to be stopping the rally in gold.

Reaching session highs at $4,630, the frantic buying is stalling a bit.

For pullbacks, aggressive pullback buying could take place at $4,590 (intraday channel lows).

Breaking the steep session channel would point to a retest of the previous all-time highs around $4,550.

Levels to watch for gold (XAUUSD:CUR) trading:

Resistance levels

  • $4,630 current session and all-time high
  • $4,660 to $4,670 potential resistance
  • Potential Mini-Resistance 2 $4,700-4,720
  • Top of daily channel and psychological level $5,000

Support levels

  • Intraday channel lows $4,590
  • Previous ATH pivot (as support) $4,500-4,550
  • Session lows previous ATH pivot (as support) $4,500-4,550
  • Major intraday support $4,400 and 4H 50-MA
  • December 31 mini-support $4,280
  • Weekly major pivot $3,950-4,000

Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) Daily and 2H Charts

Daily Chart

The action in XAG/USD is starting to now look insane.

Silver is leading its peers yet again in today's action, up around 8% in a huge bullish candle in today's action, easily breaching beyond the $85 psychological level, leaving more technical upside to the rally.

Also forming a daily channel, no resistance is emerging before the $88-89 Fibonacci area.

Except for any major fundamental change (like world peace or a return of Fed hikes), nothing is looking to stop the run.

Let's see what the intraday charts are telling us.

2H Chart and Technical Levels

With the action now stalling a bit after the tumultuous overnight/morning action, silver traders will be looking at two technical elements:

  • Whether bulls manage to fully break above the sideways channel (testing a breakout)
  • Or follow the steep tight bull channel from the current session, pointing to even more aggressive action.
    • Breaking below this one hints at a retest of the $80.00 momentum pivot.
    • Maintaining the momentum would easily guide the action to $88 and potentially more.

To put things into perspective, silver is up 77% since Williams' comments hinted at a December Fed cut!

Levels to watch for silver (XAGUSD:CUR) trading:

Resistance Levels:

  • $86.23 session and all-time highs
  • Potential Mini-Resistance 1 $87 to $89
  • Potential Mini-Resistance and Psychological Level 2 $90 to $92

Support Levels:

  • $82 to $84 previous ATH pivot (as support)
  • $80.00 momentum pivot
  • $75 to $77 minor support and channel lows
  • December 31 lows $70.00
  • Pre-FOMC major support $58.00-60 and 50-Day MA

Safe Trades, and a successful week!
